docs.title: Azure AKS Practical Guide
docs.text:
Comprehensive guide for running containerized applications on Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) — from first deployment to production operations.

graph LR
    A[Developer] --> B[Container Registry]
    B --> C[AKS Cluster]
    C --> D[Node Pools]
    D --> E[Pods]
    E --> F[Services]
    F --> G[Ingress]
    G --> H[Users]

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How AKS architecture works
  • Best practices for production workloads
  • Day-2 operations and maintenance
  • Troubleshooting common issues

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Create an AKS cluster
az aks create \
    --resource-group myResourceGroup \
    --name myAKSCluster \
    --node-count 3 \
    --enable-managed-identity \
    --generate-ssh-keys

# Get credentials
az aks get-credentials \
    --resource-group myResourceGroup \
    --name myAKSCluster

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Platform Overview
  • Best Practices
  • Troubleshooting

docs.title: Sources
docs.text: • Azure Kubernetes Service documentation

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text: Production-ready patterns for running workloads on Azure Kubernetes Service.

docs.title: Use Managed Identity
docs.text:
az aks create \
    --resource-group myResourceGroup \
    --name myAKSCluster \
    --enable-managed-identity

docs.title: Enable Azure Policy
docs.text:
az aks enable-addons \
    --addons azure-policy \
    --resource-group myResourceGroup \
    --name myAKSCluster

docs.title: Set Resource Requests and Limits
docs.text:
resources:
  requests:
    memory: "128Mi"
    cpu: "250m"
  limits:
    memory: "256Mi"
    cpu: "500m"

docs.title: Use Pod Disruption Budgets
docs.text:
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: app-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: myapp

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• Enable Azure AD integration
  • Use network policies
  • Scan container images
  • Rotate credentials regularly

docs.title: Sources
docs.text: • AKS best practices

docs.title: Operations
docs.text: Day-2 operational tasks for managing AKS clusters in production.

docs.title: Manual Scaling
docs.text:
# Scale node pool
az aks scale \
    --resource-group myResourceGroup \
    --name myAKSCluster \
    --node-count 5

# Scale deployment
kubectl scale deployment myapp --replicas=5

docs.title: Cluster Autoscaler
docs.text:
az aks update \
    --resource-group myResourceGroup \
    --name myAKSCluster \
    --enable-cluster-autoscaler \
    --min-count 1 \
    --max-count 10

docs.title: Check Available Versions
docs.text:
az aks get-upgrades \
    --resource-group myResourceGroup \
    --name myAKSCluster \
    --output table

docs.title: Upgrade Cluster
docs.text:
az aks upgrade \
    --resource-group myResourceGroup \
    --name myAKSCluster \
    --kubernetes-version 1.28.0

docs.title: Enable Container Insights
docs.text:
az aks enable-addons \
    --addons monitoring \
    --resource-group myResourceGroup \
    --name myAKSCluster

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Platform Overview
  • Troubleshooting

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Scale AKS cluster
  • Upgrade AKS cluster

docs.title: Platform Overview
docs.text:
Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies deploying, managing, and scaling containerized applications.

graph TB
    subgraph "Azure Subscription"
        subgraph "AKS Cluster"
            CP[Control Plane<br/>Managed by Azure]
            subgraph "Node Pool"
                N1[Node 1]
                N2[Node 2]
                N3[Node 3]
            end
        end
        ACR[Container Registry]
        KV[Key Vault]
        LA[Log Analytics]
    end
    CP --> N1
    CP --> N2
    CP --> N3
    ACR --> N1
    ACR --> N2
    ACR --> N3

docs.title: Control Plane
docs.text:
The control plane is fully managed by Azure. It includes:

  • API Server
  • etcd (cluster state)
  • Scheduler
  • Controller Manager

docs.title: Node Pools
docs.text:
Node pools are groups of VMs that run your workloads. You can have:

  • System node pools — Run critical system pods
  • User node pools — Run application workloads

docs.title: Networking
docs.text:
AKS supports multiple networking models:

  • Kubenet — Basic networking with NAT
  • Azure CNI — Pods get VNet IP addresses
  • Azure CNI Overlay — Overlay network for large clusters

docs.text:

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Diagnose and resolve common issues in Azure Kubernetes Service.

flowchart TD
    A[Issue Detected] --> B{Pod Running?}
    B -->|No| C[Check Events]
    B -->|Yes| D{Service Reachable?}
    C --> E[ImagePullBackOff?]
    C --> F[CrashLoopBackOff?]
    D -->|No| G[Check Endpoints]
    D -->|Yes| H{Ingress Working?}
    E --> I[Fix Registry Auth]
    F --> J[Check Logs]
    G --> K[Fix Service Selector]
    H -->|No| L[Check Ingress Config]
    H -->|Yes| M[Issue Resolved]

docs.title: Pod Not Starting
docs.text:
# Check pod status
kubectl get pods

# Check events
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

# Check logs
kubectl logs <pod-name>

docs.title: Service Not Reachable
docs.text:
# Check service
kubectl get svc

# Check endpoints
kubectl get endpoints <service-name>

# Test from within cluster
kubectl run test --rm -it --image=busybox -- wget -qO- <service-name>

docs.title: Node Issues
docs.text:
# Check node status
kubectl get nodes

# Check node conditions
kubectl describe node <node-name>

# Check node resource usage
kubectl top nodes
